speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's fine, thanks. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Monday, December the 4th, 1995. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Henia Goldman. We're in Sutton, Surrey, England, and this is tape two. You were saying about the Russians, that the Russians then came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So after this experience of the German experience for the two weeks, when the Russians came, they were really welcomed. Also, we were apprehensive, because we didn't know very much about the Russian background. And I remember we tried to ask the soldiers who were occupying, how life is, what is Russia? And they were all full of their propaganda and they were painting a picture that is a paradise, you know. It's won--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came home to my father. I said, don't worry. It's going to be lovely. Life is going to be wonderful. I didn't realize.

speaker: interviewee
text: But something was not quite right, because the first day they arrived, all the shops were just emptied of everything. It wasn't a very rich town. It didn't have anything special. But everything, they bought whatever there was. And it was like a hol-- everything emptied.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was this, this dilemma. Why is it-- if it's so good, why is this Polish little town has more to offer them, they buy anything which it is? Later on we learned.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, in one way, what it has done for me was that I could go back to education. My father then couldn't resist. He couldn't object anymore. And the Russians have introduced the secondary education. You know, they have 10 years school, from 8 to 18. So they took the seventh class and called it the seventh class-- also the standard was very low-- but they introduced it. And so I could go to second education. I did mention to you that my father was objecting. And as I was so very unhappy, we compromised in 1938/9, that I used to have private lessons from a teach-- from a student. And then the arrangement was at the end of the year I'll go to Lvov and take the exam in gymnasium. As a matter of fact, this teacher is still alive. And I met him in New York when I visited. He was the one who opened the doors for me to Greek and to literature and to history, and-- he was a student himself, but he needed extra money to maintain himself, and he was teaching. So I was relieved that at least now I'd be able to educate myself. Education was very important, because it was a way out of this little town and opportunity for future. If I tell my grandchildren that most of the students who managed-- which I envied them, that they managed to get to gymnasium, the station was about 1 and 1/2 kilometers away from the town. And they used to get up for the 8 o'clock train. So they had to walk, [? or ?] because they couldn't afford traveling-- there were no cars in our town. There were [NON-ENGLISH], or in the winter sleigh. And then they would come back at night. And then they would go every day, sleigh-- rain, snow, and [INAUDIBLE]. And they considered themselves lucky to have the opportunity to do. It would be hardship nowadays to consider. But they've considered themselves-- and I envied them that they had the opportunity to do. The emphasis was a great drive, a great emphasis on education. Because it was, one, not only-- I mean, Jewish families always put emphasis on learning and teaching, and-- But also, it was a way out of this environment and a door to the world outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russian came and they formed this sort of secondary education, I was relieved. And I joined it and I was very happy. I promised my father that I will go Saturday to-- to school, because we had to. I mean, one of the Russian method was they wanted to break down the religious attitudes. So I would go to school. I would write. I will go dressed up. I won't carry any books, because carrying was forbidden. And I kept it, and-- because I promised him that I'll do that. So for me, education-- my father, there again was my first test of him. He-- of course, everything was nationalized. As my family was regarded to be the better off, the rich ones, my grandmother and my aunt went to Lvov to live incognito. We were left in Sadowa Wisznia. We also were able to supply them with food, because bigger towns were more difficult and we were staying put, because out of the three, we were the less rich. You know, we were the-- the-- because they-- so we stayed put.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father, obviously the shop was closed. He was working-- because he didn't want to take official job, not to work on Shabbat, he was wood-cutting for a hospital. So he could do his quota on Friday, the double lot, not to work on Saturday. So he really believed in what he said. It wasn't just paying lip-- lip service. He really believed what he did. And life was relatively quiet. I mean, quite a few people, you know, who had left-wing political affiliation, who were Communist before the war, became the leaders. And quite a few Jewish people were involved in that. But we were not very much affected, except that the family broke down, in a sense. My grandmother and the two unmarried, my uncle and aunt with her, and my married aunt with a family moved to Lvov and they lived there incognito. We were safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incognito, you mean without sort of hiding them-- by hiding their Jewish identity, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't have-- they were not hiding Jewish. They were hiding the fact that they were well off, they were rich, because one of my uncle who was in Lvov, a very well-known personality and politically, a [NON-ENGLISH] in Lvov, he was taken to Siberia. And he died without we knowing where he was, because he was considered to be the bourgeoisie. And also he represented a [NON-ENGLISH], so politically, and he was arrested. The family was then in hiding. But there wasn't this pressure. You cannot compare-- I mean, at that time, part of my father's family who moved from the Polish part-- the German part of Poland to this, there was in January, I think '41, there was the Russian have taken all the refugees, knocked at the door, put them on the train, taken them to Siberia. At that time, it was a major tragedy. You can imagine Siberia, the images we had. And they were mostly refugees from-- from Germany, you know, who came to here. But they just didn't consider anything. They were politically suspect for them and they didn't consider whether Jews or not Jews. And they were taken on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this part of the family survived. You know, this is my father's two sisters who with part of the family. One sister whose family was broken, they survived in Siberia. And then what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friedman. And I met them after the war, because we used to send them parcels. And I wanted-- I knew that they were alive, and so I wanted to make contact. So I didn't know how to, but the only way-- I knew they lived in Sanok, which is another town. So when I came back to Poland, I wrote a letter to the-- to the-- to the town hall that I'm looking for these people, because I knew that they will come back. And I made contact with them and I'm in touch with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened next to your own family in Sadowa Wisznia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Russian-- you know, the war between Germany and Russia broke out in June '41, 22nd of June. And we were on the border, so the first day the German came out straightaway. And of course, this was a different German army who came and a different attitude. The first year, we prepared a lot of food. We knew that war, any war-- we didn't know about the war. But as soon as came in, the Germans came in, we tried to-- to save-- to-- to have stock of food. The first winter was bearable. There was [INAUDIBLE]. Well, most men were taken to labor and also the women. I used to have to go to work every day. We used to come to Judenrat and we used to be sent to different work, cleaning the streets, snow in the winter, cleaning up from the snow, carrying some ammunition at the railway station, working all kind. Cleaning offices, that was the main thing for me. And you could see my school friends sitting, because they were mostly Ukrainian who were now in charge. The Ukrainian collaborated, because the German promised them a independent Ukraine. They didn't keep their promise. But because of that, they collaborated with Germany very much and they could vent their native-- the inherent anti-Semitism was opportunity for expressing it. And they had the power. I remember very much there was a teacher that his wife taught me from my childhood. And when he-- when the Russian came in, he-- because he was a nationalist, he didn't have a job under Poland. Then he became-- he had a job and he was a mathematician. He was very proud of me. Every exhibition, every visit used to show off with me how-- how good his teaching was. During the war, he didn't recognize me. He just walked-- somebody who knew me from childhood. His wife used to always invite me because she was very proud of my achievement. He was very-- This is-- there was a change completely. And in the offices, you know, some of my school friends would sit behind the office and command me, doing it again and again. It was a kind of humiliation part. And-- and the Judenrat, this is a subject on its own, you know. It's very controversial. The people blame a lot of things. I understand how it happened. We always had a Judenrat you know. The community always had a-- a Judenrat, which were the elders of the town who-- During the German, they didn't realize. They elected. My father refused to be elected to that committee. But a lot of people went into it, with quite innocently trying-- it needed organizing, because the German would come in and demand. If an administrator came in, we had to give up our furniture or anything what they demanded to give, so you need to have a go-between.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they started in an innocent way to be the-- the go-between, to-- to represent the community before the German. Later on, some of them have became corrupt. It's understandable. If you have to make a list of people who have to go on an action, you want to protect your family first. It's very human. You understand it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a lot of bad feeling about it and a bad record. But you have to understand the process how it started. It started with the best intention to be-- to represent the German. And in circumstances it was very difficult to-- to-- to-- to remain noble [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know people who were on the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was that for you going and being kind of given orders by them to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was accepted, you know. I mean, a lot of-- we as a family, if they demanded something, we understood. We gave. But you must also realize that what we gave up was a lifeline, because very often the only way to maintain yourself was bartering for the things you have at home, for food. You see, in ghettos, in bigger towns, you had a bigger organization. You had some rations. In little town, you had very little, you know. You-- you really depended entirely on yourself. And we had no rations. We had no supplies. Restricted movement. We didn't have-- we were not allowed to go outside. We all had to wear, obviously, the-- the-- the band. And after 6 o'clock, there was a curfew. We are not allowed to go outside. And the only way to maintain yourself and to feed yourself was to barter, to go to the villages and to exchange.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as I say, the first few months were still reasonable, because we had some stock. But later on, poverty and hunger was terrible. I remember that the smell of food-- I used to faint when I used to work in the German family cleaning, you know, because we were allocated different-- and they were cooking, nothing special, just ordinary mincemeat or stew. The smell of food, I used to faint because we were so hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time, as I told you, my father was already in concentration camp, so I had the responsibility-- my grandmother came back because it was easier to be in Sadowa Wisznia at that time than in Lvov, so she came back with the unmarried sister-- daughter. So I had both of families to look after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after work, I used to take off my arm band and go to the village, which was-- anybody could shoot me. It was on-- on-- on a death sentence, because you were forbidden and there was no but. Human life had-- didn't represent any value.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the moods, if they didn't like your face. They could hit you, they-- There wasn't-- you-- you didn't have any sense of protection of the law or justice. You were completely in the hand of-- of-- of-- of the Germans or the Ukrainian police who was there. But necessity was so great that I just used to take off the band and used to go to a village and exchange whatever we had, soap or clothes, whatever, a tablecloth, anything, for a pound of grain, for a half of loaf of bread. And I used to come back home and my mother used to mince the grain on the coffee machine. We used to grind and make some soup. And this was a daily occurrence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took the-- the risk one had to in order to survive it. In bigger town, which I've learned later, you know, they were-- they were better organized. They had some schools. They had not very much, but at least-- In small towns like Sadowa Wisznia, you depended entirely on your own resources, and you were forbidden. I mean, you had to go to work every day. Did-- were you aware of the dangers when you were doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course you had--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did it because--

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to. There was no other way out of it. As I told you, my mother collapsed in a way. I mean, she was too gentle to be able to fight back. She was not equipped. She was not tough enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your young-- your sisters were too young.

speaker: interviewee
text: And too young. And my father was away. So I had the responsibility. And it was normal. It was just one of the things we had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And although you had-- your family had run a grocery shop, did-- did that give you any kind of connections for getting sort of extra supplies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. We may have a little bit more food. Soap was a very-- because generally there was-- also a bad harvest. And so there was generally a shortage of food. It was only-- but for us it was much worse, of course. So we had some things to barter with, you know, anything, clothes, anything just-- And this is where I had a contact. It was a village not far-- it was called [POLISH]-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] where I had-- Olga Demczyszak was my friend, school friend. And she was my first step. I used to go to her and barter through her. And this is where I used to go every day, every-- when the necessity was. And just last visit when I was in Israel, on the way to [POLISH], there was a Jewish family living a little bit outside. I always stopped with her. Her name was Henia Roner. She's still alive, quite a lady of 90. And she remembers me from that time, because I used to use her as a stop, you know. She would give me a hot drink or something. And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] and she always remembers how pessimistic I was at that time, already realizing. When you look back, you realize how methodical, how systematic, how perfidious everything was done. We didn't realize. It was a process continuous from '41 to '42 of dehumanizing you through hunger, through humiliation, fear, and ignorance. We were cut off. We didn't know what was happening in the next town. We were not allowed to use the post. We were not allowed to travel. We didn't have any contact with the world outside. We were completely isolated. So a lot was ignorance, but a lot was methodical humiliation, you know, breaking up any spirit you had. You know, when people talk to me, why did you go like lambs to the slaughter, it's very difficult to explain-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [DRINKS] --the process which was done. I only see it now. I didn't see it then. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECOND] 1st of May, 1942, I think it's a national holiday, a Socialist national [BACKGROUND NOISE] holiday in Germany. So we were all gathered together in the marketplace through the Judenrat. We had all to come to the marketplace with everybody, ill, children, oldest, with everything-- we could just pack a packet.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a pronouncement that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pronouncement beforehand. It was told to us. And we all came. And we didn't know what was going to happen, but we all came because the police was going around. We had to come. And what they have done on that particular-- I remember it was Sunday, a very sunny, beautiful day. During the war, the '39, a building-- three buildings, [INAUDIBLE] cottages, were destroyed in one end of the town. So they decreed that we have to carry the bricks from this point to the other side to the town. You know, we only had one main street. And we walked with the bricks and deposited them. It had no function. It had no reason. It had no-- it was-- and the German was on a horse riding between and hitting and with his [NON-ENGLISH]. The people coming back from the church stood and laughed and enjoyed the performance. It had no-- any political-- political-- we didn't understand what was happening. But now I look back, it was a sort of preparation, you know, first with this humiliation. And at the end of the day they sent us all home. So the next day, when the-- when the same decree came, we went with confidence. But this was the Final Solution.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thinking the same thing would happen again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was already the Final Solution. You see, that-- that we didn't know about the Final Solution. We didn't know. There were rumors. But who could have believed it? Even now it's difficult to believe it. We didn't-- we didn't know what was happening outside. We didn't know. We thought that we were going to be taken to a different place where we will be able to work. And everybody was almost glad to go, because the hunger and the deprivation was so terrible at that stage, 1942-- it was October, after the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that we almost gladly wanted to go somewhere where we thought we will work. At least we will have rations. We will eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: It had to be better than what you had at the time.

words.text: than what you had at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It couldn't be worse. So we all went. And this was already a different atmosphere. We could realize this straightaway. Took us into the synagogues. You know, I told you there were three synagogue, that this was the-- the-- the-- the gathering center where they collected us all. We all sort of had hiding spaces in our houses. We sort of thought to prevent, naively, to do something, so we all-- my father was at that time back. What happened was we bought him out from concentration camp. It used to be possible still at the beginning. As a matter of fact, he had signals to say that he will be taken out. But he was caught, and the German in Janowska showed on how they treat people who try to escape. So you can imagine what kind of hiding he had, but he was a very strong man. He came back a very broken man, but we did manage to-- to-- to buy him out. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About that summer, '40-- '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always remember this incident, and I quote it, what kind of family we were. At that time, you didn't know what your next movement will be, whether you won't need this to buy out your own husband or your own son. Yet, the whole family contributed to buying him out. It was remarkable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kind of spirit you don't feel you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was dangerous, you see. It's very easy to-- it's only when you are tested that you realize that your true character comes out. And so-- so we were-- yes. So we all had different hiding places. My father was taken to work, so he was taken from that point of view. My mother and my younger sister were hiding in one place. I with my other sister were hiding in the other place. And of course they were going on and searching. It was-- it lasted three days, the action. So they were going through. They didn't find anybody. They were going through. And we had this sort of hay, although it was town, but in the little hiding space. So I could hear a German coming and probing with a-- with his bayonet. So I walked out, thinking that my sister would remain there. And when I came to the synagogue, to the meeting place, I was on my own. I said, oh, fine. They are still there, you know. They are hiding. But gradually, gradually, as it was three days, I found my grandmother, my aunt, my sister. But my mother and my younger sister were not there. Over this-- And then eventually they asked us to walk to the station. I think I mentioned to you the station was about 1 and 1/2 or 2 kilometers from town, and we had to walk. And this was already-- I could see it coming, you know. First of all, the shooting was going on all along, anybody who couldn't keep space-- pace. And then in front of us, the first day they shot the Judenrat, you know, the main dealer, wheeler, who used to be the god in a way, you know, who could-- who-- who was the go-between. He was shot straightaway, so we straightaway realize, you know, how serious-- whether this is the end. And as we were walking, people were shot on the way. And we came to the train, and then I met-- my younger sister was taken in the last minute where we were loaded into the trains. And she came to me and said, don't be angry with me. I won't cry, you know. I-- the story was, which I later find out, that as my mother didn't hear anything going on, she went out and the child ran in the street and she was caught in the last minute, brought in the last day when we were loaded to the train, whereas my mother then realize what's happened, went to the police and-- and volunteered. The train was already gone, so she was left in Sadowa Wisznia, out of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the train, I was with my grandmother, with my aunt, with my two sisters. And I remember-- I told you the incident when I ask my grandmother, why you? She was always ailing. She had a heart condition. She was very delicate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always, you know, everybody sort of worshiped her and she was a very delicate person. And she was in the train. And she said, God wishes like that to the last minute. And then we really didn't know where we were going. We were-- we passed Janowska. We passed Lvov, because it was lit up. It was at night. And this is where they deposited, they segregated the younger men who were still working age.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father and my aunt and my uncle were deposited-- I didn't know at that time-- They were somewhere else on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: On Janowska.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train, yes. So they were deposited in Janowska, and we were going. The slow train was moving very slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: The train was like a cattle train? Was it very--

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: --crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I went to Washington museum and I saw the-- the museum. And I went to the train and I walked through. Nothing conveys what-- you know, it's sanitized. It doesn't-- I know it performs a very good function, and I think it's important for the future generation. But it's difficult to describe in words what was going on. We didn't have toilet facilities. We didn't have-- there were mixture of young, old, children. Whenever I was feeding my child, I always felt the child fed in the train where it was crying and blood almost sucking. It was-- it was tough. We were bewildered. We just didn't know. We were hoping we're going somewhere better. But you could see they were old, new, children, every-- the younger working people were taken off. But-- but we were-- we were helpless. People came to the station to glory-- to-- to-- to watch the performance, because it was October, it was sunny day, and enjoying the performance. These were people we were neighbors, lived together for so many years together.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was another part of the-- the sort of-- the humiliating,

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: The demoralizing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the attitude of the people. Probably some of them didn't, but some of them enjoyed the performance and came out. And we were already three days without any food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my aunt suggested as we were passing Lvov-- because part of the family we still had in Lvov, and in [NON-ENGLISH]-- said, you know, we ought to let them know where we are going. And-- and she suggested that since I have always been the organizer of the family, that I try to get out to notify them where we are going. This moment is with me all my life, because I just try to analyze how much of it was my self-- instinct for self-preservation, that I wanted to live, and how much it was that I wanted to notify? And also, I knew my mother was still there and I wanted to be with her, because I knew that she needed to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I jumped out of this train. I was very slim at that time. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And why didn't I take my sister with me? Tell me. She was younger than I. She was blonde.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had all the attributes to survive, because if you were blonde and blue eyes, you had a certificate-- not for survival, but it was easier to survive than you-- if you had Semitic features. I was very looking Jewish, feeling Jewish. She didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't I take her with? I don't know. I try to live with this moment all my life. [CRYING] I don't think I went through life-- Stop.
